g.text: PROOF 1: Slow-Fast Pointer Finds the Middle of Linked List
Claim: When fast (2x speed) reaches the end, slow is at the middle.
slow is at node 3 → ✅ That's the MIDDLE!
Let n = total number of nodes.
• slow moves 1 step → distance_slow = k (after k iterations)
• fast moves 2 steps → distance_fast = 2k (after k iterations)
fast stops when it reaches the end: 2k = n → k = n/2
At that point, slow has moved k = n/2 steps.
n/2 is exactly the middle index! ∎
For even n: slow lands on the 2nd middle (standard convention).
PROOF 2: Floyd's Cycle Detection — Finding the Cycle Start
Claim: After slow==fast, reset slow to head. Move both by 1. They meet at cycle start.
m = distance from HEAD to CYCLE START
k = distance from CYCLE START to MEETING POINT
L = total length of the cycle
(L - k) = distance from MEETING POINT back to CYCLE START (remaining cycle)
STEP 1 — Distances when slow & fast MEET:
• fast traveled: m + k + n·L (fast went around the cycle n times)
STEP 2 — Fast moves at 2× speed of slow:
STEP 3 — What does m = n·L - k mean?
→ A pointer starting at MEETING POINT and walking m steps
will complete (n-1) full cycles, then walk (L-k) more steps,
which lands it EXACTLY at the CYCLE START!
→ A pointer starting at HEAD and walking m steps
also lands at the CYCLE START (by definition of m).
1. slow = head ← Reset slow back to head
2. Move BOTH by 1 step at a time:
3. When slow == fast → That node is the CYCLE START! ✅
Why? Because both walk exactly m steps to reach cycle start.
(slow walks m from head, fast walks m = (n-1)L + (L-k) inside cycle) ∎
Step 3: slow=4, fast=4 ← slow == fast! Cycle detected at node 4.
PHASE 2 — Find cycle start (reset slow to head, both +1):
slow == fast at node 2 → ✅ CYCLE START FOUND!
Verify: m = 1 step from head to cycle start.
Both pointers took exactly 1 step to meet at node 2. ∎
